course_description:
This course provides students with an understanding of the policies, practices and politics of disaster and crisis management. The course locates contemporary disasters and crises within domestic, international and developing contexts and it applies development, public policy and political science lenses to understand them. This allows students to think practically about the ways in which disasters can be managed and to think critically about the ways in which disaster management ought to proceed. Students will apply their practical and critical thinking to the exploration of vulnerability, resilience and crisis planning, leadership, decision making and emergency responses, and modes of reform and recovery that are participatory and emancipatory in nature.

This course introduces students to the world of crisis andﾠdisaster management with a specific emphasis on developing contexts and international comparison. We will examine if/how nations can reduce vulnerability to disasters and strengthen pre-crisis preparedness. We will examine the extraordinary challenges that await the institutions and agents that are tasked with responding to disasters during the emergency stage. And we will examine the dynamics of post-crisis reform, accountability and blame which characterise aftermath reconstruction periods. Through discussion of an eclectic range of literatures (from public policy, governance, development, geography, anthropology and more) and analysis of a range of topical case studies (for example, Haitian and Pakistan earthquakes, Asian Tsunamis, Bangladesh floods, Ugandan landslides, Victoria Bushfires and of course COVID-19) students will be equipped with a practical and critical insight into crisis and disaster management. This course requires a serious investment both in terms of weekly readings and completion of assessment. Prospective students wishing to know more can email alastair.stark@uq.edu.au to receive a copy of last year's learning guide.
timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
This course comprises 12 weeks of learning which includes lectures, tutorials and workshops. These activities take place during the scheduled timeslot on Thursdays afternoons.ﾠ
course_aims: This course has three core aims:

learning_outcomes.description: 1. Demonstrate an accurate critical understanding - 1. Demonstrate an accurate critical understanding of contemporary disasters and their impacts in various international contexts.

1. Demonstrate an accurate critical understanding - 2. Investigate, diagnose and evaluate the causes of disaster with particular reference to weaknesses caused by development practice (or lack thereof).

1. Demonstrate an accurate critical understanding - 3. Critically appraise efforts to resolve disaster emergencies in developing nations. Problematize and reflect upon the unique leadership challenges of crisis response in a development context.
1. Demonstrate an accurate critical understanding - 4. Analyse the dynamics of post-crisis change and inertia with a specific emphasis on politics and participatory governance.

assessment_details.title: Reading Group Discussion and Dissemination
assessment_details.task_description: This course is driven by students discussing literature and teaching others about it. In recognition of this there is a substantial participation mark associated with the course. Extensive details of expectations in terms of the reading groups are provided in the course learning guide, which is available on Blackboard. However. the essential aspects in terms of the award of assessment marks are straightforward. We have ten reading group sessions. Two marks are available for every student in each session. The remaining five marks are made through participation in the final week's lesson learning session (which also requires the submission of a short lesson learning reflection sheet). Assessment tasks are intended to evaluate a student’s abilities, skills, and knowledge. Unless explicitly stated otherwise in the assessment instructions, assessments are to be completed without the aid of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT). Failure to comply with this direction may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessment_details.title: Pre-Crisis Preparation Questions
assessment_details.task_description: The take home assessment will be sent to your UQ student email address and it should be returned electronically via Turnitin (more details will be given out in class). The assessment will contain 3 questions that reflect the pre-crisis preparedness sections of the course. These three questions will also reflect the core aims of the course. One question will demand that you engage critically with a piece of disaster management literature. A second will demand that you think about disaster management from a practitioner-orientated perspective. And the third will expect you to think critically (and independently) about a problem or debate pertaining to disaster management knowledge and practice. You are required to type a 500-word answer for each question. More details will be given out in class and can be found in the course learning guide. Assessment tasks are intended to evaluate a student’s abilities, skills, and knowledge. Unless explicitly stated otherwise in the assessment instructions, assessments are to be completed without the aid of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT). Failure to comply with this direction may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Assignments for this course will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using Turnitin .
In uploading an assignment via Turnitin you are certifying that it is your original work, that it has not been copied in whole or part from another person or source except where this is properly acknowledged, and that it has not in whole or part been previously submitted for assessment in any other course at this or any other university.
When you successfully submit your assessment to Turnitin after previewing the uploaded document (to make sure that you have chosen the correct file), you should see the “Submission Complete!” message. After this, a downloadable Digital Receipt will display on your Assignment Dashboard. It is your responsibility to download the Digital Receipt as proof of submission. Turnitin will not send this receipt to you automatically.
If you don’t see the downloadable receipt on your assignment dashboard, you should regard your submission as unsuccessful.
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again, then email your course coordinator immediately.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Unless an extension is granted, penalties for late submission apply. Students are penalised 10% of the maximum possible mark allocated for the assessment item for every calendar day that an assessment item is late.
Marks will be deducted each day for up to 7 calendar days, at which point the submitted item will not receive any marks unless an extension has been approved. Each 24-hour block is triggered from the time the submission is due.

assessment_details.title: Lesson Learning Essay (2000 words)
assessment_details.task_description: You must choose a case study of a specific crisis or disaster. You must then write an essay that: 1) summarises the case chronologically with reference to the PPRR stages; 2) presents an argument about the case which reflects a theme found in our specific course literature; 3) presents lessons for crisis management practitioners and/or researchers. More details are available in the learning guide. Assessment tasks are intended to evaluate a student’s abilities, skills, and knowledge. Unless explicitly stated otherwise in the assessment instructions, assessments are to be completed without the aid of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT). Failure to comply with this direction may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.topic: Introduction to Crisis Management In this introductory lecture I will be aiming to spark your enthusiasm for the study of crisis. The focus of the lecture will be on types of crises, the characteristics of crisis management and the way in which these events provide a lens into our (rather dystopian) future. We will also spend some time getting ready for our first reading groups which will follow in Week Two. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Crisis Management Paradigms This week the lecture will introduce three paradigms which have underpinned how researchers and policymakers have thought about crisis management for the past 40 years. These are the hazard paradigm, the vulnerability paradigm and most recently the resilience paradigm. Learning outcomes: L01, L02

Crisis Types This is our first week of combined lecture and reading group discussions. Note that the reading groups will always be discussing the topic from the previous weekﾒs lecture, which for this session is crisis types and definitions (with a specific focus on transboundary and creeping crises). We will do this session first and then the lecture on the new topic will follow. Learning outcomes: L01, L02

Pre-Crisis Planning and Preparedness In this lecture we begin to make a gear change by moving away from academic issues and moving towards practical thinking about how to prepare for crises. We do that in this lecture by working through three aspects of the pre-crisis stage. First, the preparedness cycle. Second, sensemaking processes. Finally, crisis planning, which is one of my favourite topics because it allows us to examine how modes of rational crisis management can be completely undone when they meet the real world in all its glorious uncertainty. Learning outcomes: L01, L02

Crisis Management Paradigms This week's reading groups will focus on one article each that reflect the three paradigms discussed in the lecture last week (hazards, vulnerability and resilience). Learning outcomes: L01, L02

Crisis Politics Prior to discussing the more practical elements of a crisis response I want to spend a week discussing politics because it is a fallacy to believe that politics go on hold during an emergency. Perceptions about what constitutes successful and unsuccessful crisis management, and corresponding notions of legitimacy and illegitimacy (winners and losers) are bound up with political dynamics. In this regard a crucial part of understanding crisis management performance requires us to understand how certain types of communication can, for example, reassure citizens, assert control or court controversy, allocate blame, reward heroism and ultimately determine our understanding of who is affected and how they should be helped. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Pre-Crisis Preparedness Tools Building on last week's lecture, our reading groups this week will focus on research relating to three elements of the pre-crisis tool-kit: 1) disaster education; 2) scenario building; 3) models that value and utilise indigenous knowledge in crisis planning. Learning outcomes: L01, L02

Guest Lecture and Position Statement This week we will have a guest lecturer who can talk to us about crisis planning in the real world. This is an opportunity for you to see how our theories play out in the reality of crises via an interaction with an experienced crisis manager. We will also have a 'position statement' session in which I recap what we have learned in anticipation for our first assessment. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Guest Lecture and Position Statement No reading groups this week so you can prepare for the first take home assessment. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Crisis Response Frameworks We are now entering another two-week block of teaching which will once again end in an assessment. When we talk about the ﾑacute-stageﾒ of disaster management we are using a generic term for the period in a disaster which is most demanding; a period in which individuals and organisations often need to make life or death decisions under conditions of high uncertainty. In this lecture I will focus on crisis coordination via a conceptual and empirical introduction to response frameworks, which represent the primary mechanisms for responding to crises. Learning outcomes: L01, L03

Meaning Making Reading groups this week will focus on research relating to symbolism, rhetoric, discourse and dramaturgy. These all relate to the ways in which we understand and interpret crisis events and actions. Learning outcomes: L01, L03

Participation in Emergencies This week we shift the lens from the mechanics of a crisis response to the environment in which responses have to operate. We do this by examining the different ways that crisis management scholars have analysed public participation during crises. These are important because participatory governance solutions are now very fashionable in pre-crisis planning and crisis response but we often forget that ﾑweﾒ are always the initial emergency responders in any disaster. Learning outcomes: L01, L03

Acute-Stage Practices This week our readings will allow us to focus on three aspects of acute-stage crisis management: 1) leadership via psychological studies; 2) decision making through organisational science; 3) cluster coordination. Learning outcomes: L01, L03

The Post-Crisis Stage: Learning v Accountability We are now moving on to examine the post-crisis recovery period. Many people believe that when the acute-stage ends the disaster or crisis is over. However, crisis researchers know that post-crisis periods often share all of the characteristics of the preceding stage in the sense that they are uncertain, highly dynamic and require decision making with serious consequences attached. In this environment practitioners need to get to grips with the tension between learning and accountability and the politics of recovery and reform. This is the focus of this lecture. Learning outcomes: L01, L04

Participation in Emergencies This week's readings take us into uncomfortable territory through a series of criticisms attached to the issue of participation. We tend to view our involvement in decision making as a good thing but in crises our participation can create cultural conflict, stress and trauma and it can take resources away from essential activities. These three readings invite us to reflect on these issues in terms of humanitarian assistance, the darker side of community resilience and the negative character of emergent groups. Learning outcomes: L01, L03
Mid-Semester Break (No Class)

Guest Lecture and Position Statement This week we will have a guest lecturer who can talk to us about emergency decision making in the real world. This is our second opportunity to explore our theories with an experienced crisis manager. Once again we also prep for the next take home assessment this week. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Guest Lecture and Position Statement No reading groups this week so you can prepare for the first take home assessment. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Post-Crisis Recovery: Social Capital This week we will explore the role of social capital in relation to recovery and specifically focus (once again) on how participatory mechanisms can leverage community efforts to collectively recover from crises. I am hoping to be joined by a recovery practitioner this week who will help us bridge theory and practice in terms of this stage. Learning outcomes: L01, L04

Lesson Learning Reading groups this week will be focusing on how we learn from crises. We will first look at the novel topic of intra-crisis learning, then explore the public inquiry which is the dominant mechanism for learning from crises and we will also examine dynamic capacity, which is a new concept designed to help us understand how post-crisis lessons travel around agencies, sectors and nations. Learning outcomes: L01, L04

Overview This is the final lecture and at this point in the semester, I will be keeping it brief! A short synopsis of what we have learned so far will prep us for next week's lessons learned workshop. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Social Capital and Participation In this final round of readings we have two cases - the Kobe earthquake and the Indian Ocean tsunami - through which we will explore the value of social capital in terms of recovery and successful models for empowering communities after devastating events. Learning outcomes: L01, L04
Lessons Learned Workshop In this final week we will summarise what we have learned from the course and from our research on our lesson learning essays. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
